text: The surgeon demonstrates a laparoscopic fundoplication with crural repair for a type three hiatal hernia. The lower esophagus, cardia, and fundus of the stomach are visualized in the mediastinum. The stomach is pulled back to examine the anatomy. The phrenoesophageal membrane is incised above the hepatic branch of the vagus nerve, preserving the nerve. The incision extends from the right to the left crus, carefully avoiding damage to the fascia and the left vagal branch. Small attachments between the stomach fundus are divided to free the fundus for a tension-free fundoplication.

The surgeon identifies the lower esophagus, cardia, and fundus of the stomach, which have migrated into the mediastinum due to a type three hiatal hernia. The stomach is pulled back to examine the anatomy. The phrenoesophageal membrane is incised, preserving the hepatic branch of the vagal nerve, while dividing the membrane from the right to the left crus. Care is taken to avoid damaging the fascia covering the crura and the left vagal branch. Small attachments between the fundus and surrounding structures are divided to free the fundus for a tension-free fundoplication.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the right and left crura and the esophagus, aiming to identify the pleura while avoiding bleeding that could obscure the surgical field. The left pleura is visible as a white line beneath the instrument. The dissection continues into the mediastinum to mobilize at least five centimeters of the esophagus intra-abdominally, which is crucial for preventing recurrence.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the right and left crura and the esophagus, aiming to identify the pleura without causing bleeding. The left pleura is visible as a white line below the instrument. The dissection continues into the mediastinum to mobilize at least five centimeters of the esophagus intra-abdominally.

The surgeon opens the omentum into two layers to identify the short gastric vessels. These vessels are coagulated and divided separately to reduce the risk of bleeding. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid thermal injury to the stomach, ensuring a floppy fundus for the fundoplication.

The surgeon begins dividing the upper branches of the short gastric vessels to ensure a floppy fundus for the fundoplication. The omentum is opened into two layers, allowing identification and coagulation of the short gastric vessels before division. Care is taken to avoid thermal injury to the stomach by not dissecting too closely.

The surgeon divides the second branch of the short gastric vessels, ensuring the fundus is free from undue retraction. The fundus is now visible, and small posterior attachments are carefully divided to avoid thermal damage. The surgeon maintains a safe distance from the stomach to prevent injury. The final attachments between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach are severed, resulting in a completely free fundus ready for fundoplication.

The surgeon divides the remaining short gastric vessels and posterior attachments to fully mobilize the fundus of the stomach. Care is taken to avoid thermal damage to the stomach and prevent injury to the splenic capsule. The fundus is now completely free, allowing for a tension-free fundoplication.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection to take down attachments and uses umbilical tape for safe manipulation of the esophagus, avoiding direct instrument contact due to edema. The dissection continues cranially into the mediastinum, revealing the beating heart and pericardium anteriorly. Careful dissection is carried out between the pleura and periesophageal tissues, aided by carbon dioxide to create a clear plane. Fibrinous attachments are sharply divided to prevent bleeding, facilitating further mobilization of the esophagus for sufficient intra-abdominal length.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection to take down attachments and uses umbilical tape for safe manipulation of the esophagus, avoiding direct instrument contact due to edema. The dissection progresses cranially into the mediastinum, revealing the beating heart and pericardium anteriorly. Careful dissection is carried out between the pleura and perisophageal tissues, aided by carbon dioxide to expand the dissection plane. Fibrinous attachments are sharply divided to prevent bleeding, ensuring sufficient intra-abdominal length of the esophagus for successful postoperative outcomes.

The surgeon continues cranial dissection, removing attachments between the tissue surrounding the pericardium and the esophagus. The beating heart is visible to the left of the instrument. A circumferential mobilization of the esophagus is performed, with the pleura clearly visible on the left. An umbilical tape is used to measure the dissection, showing 11 centimeters of esophagus mobilized, with 6 to 6.5 centimeters moved into the abdomen. The posterior plane is opened, revealing the left and right crura, and small fibrous tissues between the esophagus and posterior area are carefully dissected to avoid damaging the right vagus nerve.

The surgeon performs cranial dissection, removing attachments between the tissue surrounding the pericardium and the esophagus. The beating heart is visible to the left of the instrument. A circumferential mobilization of the esophagus is achieved, with the pleura clearly visible on the right. An umbilical tape is used to measure the dissection, showing 11 centimeters of esophagus mobilized, with 6 to 6.5 centimeters brought into the abdomen. The left and right crura are dissected, and small fibrous tissues between the esophagus and posterior area are carefully removed, avoiding damage to the right vagus nerve.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the esophagus, revealing the aorta just below the instrument. The left and right crura are visible, with the beating heart and pericardium seen anteriorly. The esophagus is fully mobilized, and the surgeon takes a suture bite on the left crura, ensuring it includes the fascia for strength. The proximity of the aorta and inferior vena cava requires careful handling to avoid damage. The surgeon uses a monofilament suture for its handling properties, taking additional bites as needed while protecting the liver from needle injury.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the aorta, visible below the instrument, ensuring not to extend too posteriorly. The left and right crura are visible, with the beating heart and pericardium seen anteriorly. The esophagus has been fully mobilized, crucial for the procedure's success. The surgeon takes a suture bite on the left crus, ensuring it includes the fascia for strength, while avoiding vital structures like the IVC. Monofilament sutures are preferred for their handling capacity, and the surgeon carefully controls the needle to prevent damage to the liver.

The surgeon completes suturing the hiatal defect, ensuring the gap is small and adequately approximated. The umbilical tape used for esophageal manipulation is released, revealing minimal space between the esophagus and hiatus. The mobilized fundus is brought through the posterior window, showing the divided upper short gastric vessels and demonstrating the fundus's free movement without tension.

The surgeon releases traction on the umbilical tape, allowing the esophagus to rest naturally, revealing minimal space between the esophagus and the hiatus. The mobilized fundus is brought through the posterior window, showing no undue tension. The shoeshine maneuver is performed to confirm the fundus's mobility, ensuring it remains in position without retraction. Bites are taken on the anterior and posterior walls of the stomach to prevent twisting of the wrap.

The surgeon performs the shoeshine maneuver to ensure the fundus is freely mobile, confirming there is no undue tension. The fundus is left without being held by instruments, demonstrating it remains in position without retracting. Sutures are placed with bites on the anterior wall of the stomach on the left and the posterior wall on the right to prevent twisting of the wrap. The surgeon tightens the bites, creating a floppy Nissen fundoplication with a short wrap of approximately 2 to 2.5 centimeters. The second bite includes the esophageal wall to prevent wrap migration, while the third bite involves only the stomach walls.

The surgeon performs a floppy Nissen fundoplication, creating a short wrap of approximately 2 to 2.5 centimeters. The second suture includes the esophageal wall to prevent wrap migration, while the third suture involves only the stomach walls. The wrap is positioned at the level of the esophagogastric junction, ensuring it is not below this point. The surgeon checks the wrap's floppiness by passing an instrument through it and anchors the wrap to the right crus to prevent early migration. The wrap and crural repair are inspected for hemostasis and proper positioning.

The surgeon carefully controls the needle tip to prevent damage to the liver while taking a bite. The wrap is lifted to check for adequate space for instrument passage, confirming the wrap's floppiness. The wrap measures approximately two to two and a half centimeters, which is crucial to prevent dysphagia. The wrap is anchored to the right crus to prevent early migration post-operatively. The surgeon inspects the wrap for hemostasis and proper positioning, ensuring a neat and secure repair.
